request_summary: Build Valhalla V3 — a completely rebuilt real-time dashboard for the Norse Agent System v2 pipeline. Features a multi-view SPA (Home, Backlog, Pipeline, Completed, Analytics) backed by a Node.js/Express API reading portfolio filesystem data. Dark theme, mobile-first, auto-refresh every 10s.

items.title: Data Ingestion and API Layer
items.outcome: A stable Node.js/Express API that reads, parses, and serves live portfolio filesystem data (state.yaml, execution-log.json, artifact markdown, odinclaw.config.yaml) to the frontend with appropriate polling and caching semantics.
scope: Express API server setup and routing
Filesystem reader for state.yaml and execution-log.json
Artifact markdown file resolution per run
Error handling for missing or malformed files
assumptions: Filesystem layout and file formats are stable enough to define a response schema without a discovery spike
No authentication is required for the API in v3
The API is local-only and not exposed publicly
items.split_rationale: Isolated to backend concerns; no frontend work is included. File parsing details will be decomposed in child issues.

reasons: Scope is bounded to read-only filesystem access and HTTP serialisation

items.title: Real-Time State Sync and Client Store
items.outcome: A Zustand-based client store that polls the API every 10 seconds, normalises responses, and exposes reactive slices consumed by all views — ensuring the UI always reflects current pipeline state.
scope: Zustand store architecture and slice definitions
non_goals: Persistent client-side caching beyond in-memory store
assumptions: Polling at 10s intervals is sufficient; WebSocket is out of scope for v3
All views share a single Zustand store
items.split_rationale: Separated from views so store architecture can stabilise before view-specific slices are built.

reasons: Bounded to client store wiring; no UI rendering included

items.title: Dashboard Home View
items.outcome: Users can see the currently active job at a glance via a hero card, inspect Design and Build team panels, understand pipeline flow position, and read a cost summary — all from a single home screen.
non_goals: Full pipeline stage animation (belongs to Pipeline view)
assumptions: Home view is the default landing route
Agent team membership is derived from odinclaw.config.yaml
items.split_rationale: Scoped to a single view; hero card and team panels are substantial but belong to the same user context.

reasons: Multiple components but single view boundary and single ownership

items.title: Backlog View
items.outcome: Users can browse all queued jobs, expand each job to read its full markdown description, and manually refresh the list — giving full visibility into pending work before it enters the pipeline.
assumptions: Queued jobs are identified by status in state.yaml
Markdown rendering uses an existing library (e.g. react-markdown)
items.split_rationale: Small, self-contained view; unlikely to require multiple owners.

reasons: Single view with limited interaction surface

items.title: Pipeline View
items.outcome: Users can watch a live two-phase (Design / Build) horizontal stage flow with animated job transitions, pulsing active stages, agent avatars, live duration counters, and running cost — making the in-flight execution state immediately legible.
scope: Stage card with agent avatar, duration, running cost
Animated job transitions between stages (Framer Motion)
Job card component shared with other views
assumptions: Design phase = Loki-led agents, Build phase = Thor-led agents
Stage order is deterministic and defined in odinclaw.config.yaml or state.yaml
Framer Motion is the sole animation library
items.split_rationale: Animation complexity and live data demands make this a distinct ownership boundary from other views.

reasons: Animation system, live data binding, and multi-phase layout are each substantial
Likely to span multiple PRs; child decomposition required

items.title: Completed Jobs View
items.outcome: Users can browse all completed job runs with full cost and duration breakdowns, and open a two-column stage detail overlay that renders artifact markdown — enabling post-run review and audit.
scope: Cost and duration summary per job
assumptions: Artifact markdown files are resolved from execution-log.json run paths
Overlay is a modal-style panel, not a new route
items.split_rationale: Distinct user journey (post-run review) from in-flight pipeline observation.

reasons: Two-column overlay adds complexity but stays within one view boundary

items.title: Analytics View
items.outcome: Users can assess system cost and performance trends over time via recharts visualisations, and understand budget exposure through a limits tracker with burn rate projection.
non_goals: Real-time per-job cost display (Pipeline view concern)
assumptions: Historical cost data is derivable from execution-log.json
Limits are sourced from odinclaw.config.yaml
recharts is the sole charting library
items.split_rationale: Aggregation logic and chart composition are analytically distinct from operational views.

reasons: Bounded to read-only aggregation and visualisation

items.title: Design System and Shared UI Components
items.outcome: A consistent, dark-themed, mobile-first component library (Tailwind + Framer Motion primitives) shared across all views — ensuring visual coherence and reducing per-view rework.
scope: Tailwind dark theme configuration and CSS variables
Shared layout primitives (grid, card, overlay, badge)
Animation tokens and reusable Framer Motion variants
non_goals: Storybook or visual regression testing setup
assumptions: Tailwind CSS v3 is the styling foundation
Framer Motion variants are centralised to avoid duplication
No external component library (e.g. shadcn) is mandated
items.split_rationale: Cross-cutting; consumed by all bounded context views. Must be established early to unblock parallel view development.

reasons: Scope is limited to primitives; view-specific composition lives in each BC

items.title: Application Shell, Routing, and Navigation
items.outcome: A functional SPA shell with client-side routing between all five views, persistent navigation, and global error/loading boundary — enabling independent view development and coherent user traversal.
scope: Environment configuration (API base URL, refresh interval)
assumptions: Single-page application with client-side routing only
React Router v6 is used
Five named routes: /, /backlog, /pipeline, /completed, /analytics
items.split_rationale: Shell and routing are infrastructure shared by all views; isolating them prevents coupling.

reasons: Scaffold and routing setup is well-scoped

items.title: API–Frontend Contract and Integration
items.outcome: API response shapes and TypeScript types are agreed and co-evolved between the API layer and the client store so that all views bind to stable, typed data without runtime shape mismatches.
scope: Shared TypeScript type definitions for all API responses
OpenAPI or JSON Schema for the Express endpoints
Contract validation on the client (runtime or compile-time)
assumptions: Types will be maintained in a shared types package or directory within the monorepo
No code generation toolchain is mandated initially
items.split_rationale: Integration seam between backend and frontend; neither BC owns the contract definition alone.

reasons: Contract definition is bounded and parallelisable with implementation

items.title: Filesystem Data Model Discovery
items.outcome: The exact schema of state.yaml, execution-log.json, and artifact markdown directory structure is documented and validated so that BC-01 API design and INT-01 contract definition proceed without guessing.
scope: Audit of current state.yaml fields and lifecycle statuses
Audit of execution-log.json structure per run
Artifact markdown file naming conventions and path resolution
Identification of any missing data needed by Analytics view (BC-07)
assumptions: Files are accessible in the development environment during discovery
Discovery can be completed before API implementation begins
items.split_rationale: Ambiguity in data sources blocks accurate API and contract design; discovery must precede both.

reasons: Bounded investigation; output is a schema document, not implementation
